pool.q: An auditor asks the team to prove that a specific engineer, and no one else, approved a production release last month. The team points to signed release records tied to that engineer's identity. Which security property does this MOST directly support?
pool.explanation: Non-repudiation means an actor cannot credibly deny an action they performed. A record cryptographically bound to a unique identity provides that proof. Confidentiality and availability are unrelated to attributing an action, and fault tolerance is a resilience property.
pool.tip: "Cannot deny having done it" plus a signed, identity-bound record almost always points to non-repudiation.

pool.q: A payroll service must keep running even if one data-center loses power. The team adds a second region with automatic failover. Which element of the CIA triad is this design PRIMARILY protecting?
pool.explanation: Redundancy and automatic failover keep the service reachable when a component fails, which is the definition of availability. It does not by itself protect against disclosure (confidentiality) or unauthorized modification (integrity).
pool.tip: Redundancy, failover, and backups are availability controls.

pool.q: A developer proposes storing test data by copying the production customer database and changing names and card numbers to realistic but fake values, keeping the same format so the app still works. The originals cannot be recovered from the test copy. Which technique is described?
pool.explanation: Masking/obfuscation replaces sensitive values with realistic, format-preserving substitutes so non-production systems stay usable without exposing real data. Tokenization keeps a reversible mapping in a vault, and full-disk encryption protects data at rest without changing the values.
pool.tip: Format-preserving fake data for dev/test = masking; a reversible vault mapping = tokenization.

pool.q: Which access control model enforces "no read up, no write down" using fixed labels the system assigns to both subjects and objects?
pool.explanation: MAC uses system-enforced labels and clearances; the Bell-LaPadula confidentiality rules (no read up, no write down) are classically implemented with MAC. DAC lets owners decide, RBAC bundles permissions into job roles, and ABAC evaluates attributes.
pool.tip: Fixed labels + clearances + no-read-up/no-write-down = MAC (Bell-LaPadula).

pool.q: A bank requires that issuing a large wire transfer needs one employee to initiate it and a different employee to approve it. Which principle does this BEST illustrate?
pool.explanation: Separation of duties splits a sensitive task across two or more people so no single individual can complete it alone, reducing fraud risk. Least privilege limits how much access one person has; defense in depth is layered controls; complete mediation is checking every access.
pool.tip: Two different people required to finish one sensitive action = separation of duties.

pool.q: During design review, a component is set so that if its authorization check throws an error, the request is denied rather than allowed through. Which principle is being applied?
pool.explanation: Fail-safe defaults mean the system denies by default and denies on failure, so an error results in no access rather than accidental access.
pool.tip: "On error/by default, deny" = fail-safe defaults / fail secure.

pool.q: A team consolidates many custom login flows onto one well-vetted single sign-on service so the design is small and simple enough to review and verify. Which principle drives this choice?
pool.explanation: Economy of mechanism favors simple, minimal designs that are easier to analyze and secure; consolidating to one vetted SSO reduces components and attack surface. Least common mechanism is about not sharing mechanisms between users.
pool.tip: Keywords "simple" and "minimal components" point to economy of mechanism.

pool.q: A reviewer argues that a cryptographic protocol should remain secure even if its full design is published, with only the keys kept secret. Which principle is the reviewer invoking?
pool.explanation: Open design (Kerckhoffs's principle) holds that security must not depend on secrecy of the design, only on secrecy of keys. Security through obscurity is the opposite and is weak on its own.
pool.tip: "Secure even if the design is public" = open design.

pool.q: A threat has a single loss expectancy (SLE) of $40,000 and is expected to occur about twice per year. What is the annualized loss expectancy (ALE)?
pool.explanation: ALE = SLE x ARO. With SLE = $40,000 and ARO = 2, ALE = $80,000. This figure helps justify spending on a control.
pool.tip: SLE = AV x EF and ALE = SLE x ARO.

pool.q: After applying controls, management reviews the risk that still remains and formally accepts it. What is this remaining risk called?
pool.explanation: Residual risk is what remains after controls are applied and is what management formally accepts. Inherent risk is before controls; transferred risk is shifted to another party.
pool.tip: Before controls = inherent; after controls = residual.

pool.q: A startup buys cyber-insurance to cover breach losses while keeping the risky service running. Select every statement that is TRUE about risk responses.
options: Shutting the service down would be risk avoidance
Adding controls to reduce likelihood would be risk mitigation
pool.explanation: Insurance shifts financial impact to a third party (transfer, not avoidance). Stopping the risky activity is avoidance, and applying controls is mitigation. Acceptance (not listed) means knowingly taking no action.
pool.tip: The four responses: avoid, mitigate, transfer, accept. Insurance is the classic transfer.

pool.q: In a governance hierarchy, which document is the high-level, mandatory statement of management intent that standards, procedures, and guidelines support?
pool.explanation: A policy is the top-level mandatory statement of intent. Standards specify mandatory specifics, procedures give step-by-step instructions, and guidelines are recommended, not mandatory.
pool.tip: Policy = high-level mandatory; standard = specific rules; procedure = steps; guideline = optional.

pool.q: A team keeps finding expensive security flaws just before release. A consultant recommends addressing security starting in requirements and design instead. What is this practice commonly called?
pool.explanation: Shifting left means moving security activities earlier in the lifecycle, where defects are far cheaper to fix than in production. The other options are deployment or release techniques, not a security-timing practice.
pool.tip: "Earlier in the lifecycle" = shift left.

pool.q: An organization wants a defined point in the pipeline that a release cannot pass until its threat model is approved and no critical findings remain open. What is this control called?
pool.explanation: A security gate is a checkpoint with pass/fail criteria that a project cannot proceed past until met. Retrospectives, sprint reviews, and postmortems are meetings, not enforcement gates.
pool.tip: "Cannot proceed until security criteria are met" = a gate.

pool.q: A company agrees in advance that any defect at "high" severity or above must be fixed before shipping, so deadline pressure can't quietly waive it. What is this threshold called?
pool.explanation: A bug bar is a pre-agreed severity threshold above which defects must be fixed before release, preventing ad-hoc risk acceptance under pressure. A definition of done is broader and not specifically a severity cutoff.
pool.tip: Pre-set "must fix above this severity" line = bug bar.

pool.q: How does security typically integrate into an Agile process, as opposed to Waterfall?
options: Security is a single phase after coding
Security requirements are frozen up front and never revisited
Security work is built into the definition of done and per-sprint activities
Security is handled only by an external audit at the end
pool.explanation: Agile embeds security into each iteration: security user stories, per-sprint threat modeling, and acceptance criteria in the definition of done. Freezing requirements up front and single end-phase security describe Waterfall-style or after-the-fact approaches.
pool.tip: Agile = security woven into every sprint and the definition of done.

pool.q: Compare OWASP SAMM and BSIMM. Which statement is accurate?
options: Both are prescriptive step-by-step coding standards
SAMM is prescriptive/self-assessed while BSIMM is descriptive (measures what real organizations do)
BSIMM is a static analysis tool
pool.explanation: Both are software-security maturity models. SAMM is prescriptive and self-assessed; BSIMM is descriptive, reporting what a community of real organizations actually does. Neither is a coding standard or a tool.
pool.tip: SAMM = prescriptive model; BSIMM = descriptive observed data.

pool.q: Developers embedded in each product team who receive extra security training and act as the security team's local presence are known as:
pool.explanation: Security champions scale scarce security expertise by embedding trained advocates within development teams. The other roles are specialized functions, not an in-team scaling model.
pool.tip: In-team trained security advocates = security champions.

pool.q: Why is configuration management (version control, baselines, controlled change) considered a security practice, not just an operational one?
options: It makes every change traceable and prevents uncontrolled drift that introduces vulnerabilities
It replaces the need for testing
It encrypts all data at rest
pool.explanation: Uncontrolled change is a leading cause of outages and vulnerabilities; configuration management provides traceability (who/what/when/why) and baselines so changes are reviewed and reversible. It does not replace testing or encryption.
pool.tip: Traceable, controlled change = fewer surprise vulnerabilities.

pool.q: A team is decommissioning a legacy application. Which action is essential to do securely as part of retirement?
options: Leave the credentials active in case of rollback
Revoke credentials/keys, sanitize or destroy media, and update the asset inventory
Keep all data indefinitely for convenience
Simply power off the server and move on
pool.explanation: Secure decommissioning revokes access and keys, handles data per retention rules, sanitizes or destroys media, and updates documentation and inventory. Leaving credentials active or keeping data indefinitely creates lingering risk; powering off alone is not enough.
pool.tip: Decommissioning = revoke access/keys, sanitize media, update inventory.

pool.q: A dataset must be disposed of so that even a well-resourced lab cannot recover it, but the drives will be reused internally. Which media sanitization level fits BEST?
pool.explanation: Purge (cryptographic erase or degauss) protects against laboratory recovery while allowing reuse. Clear only defeats simple recovery, and destroy prevents reuse entirely. Archive is not sanitization.
pool.tip: Reuse + lab-grade protection = purge; no reuse = destroy.

pool.q: A customer wants assurance they can still maintain critical software if the vendor goes out of business. Which contractual arrangement addresses this?
pool.explanation: Software escrow places source code with a neutral third party, released to the customer if the vendor fails, providing supply-chain continuity. SLAs govern performance, NDAs govern confidentiality, and bug bounties reward vulnerability reports.
pool.tip: "Get the source if the vendor disappears" = escrow.

pool.q: Which of the following is the BEST example of a security metric that drives improvement rather than just reporting activity?
options: Mean time to remediate (MTTR) a vulnerability, tracked over time
pool.explanation: MTTR to remediate vulnerabilities measures how quickly risk is reduced and can drive process changes. Meeting counts, lines of code, and headcount are activity or size measures, not security-outcome metrics.
pool.tip: Good metrics tie to risk reduction and drive decisions.

pool.q: What is the role of a change control board (CCB) in secure lifecycle management?
options: To review and approve significant changes, weighing security impact before deployment
pool.explanation: A CCB reviews and authorizes significant changes, assessing security and operational impact before they go live. It is a governance function, not a coding, testing, or HR role.
pool.tip: CCB = approves changes with security impact in mind.

pool.q: "All stored passwords must be hashed with a memory-hard algorithm" is BEST classified as which kind of requirement?
pool.explanation: Non-functional security requirements express qualities and constraints (how the system must be), such as how credentials are protected. A functional requirement describes a behavior the system performs, like "lock the account after 5 failed logins."
pool.tip: Quality/constraint = non-functional; a specific behavior/action = functional.

pool.q: A team writes: "As an attacker, I want to reuse a stolen session token so I can impersonate a user." What is the purpose of writing this?
options: It is a misuse/abuse case that surfaces mitigating security requirements
pool.explanation: Misuse/abuse cases describe how a hostile actor would attack the system, which drives out the security requirements that prevent them (here, token expiry and rotation). They are not marketing, ops, or performance artifacts.
pool.tip: Attacker-perspective scenario = misuse/abuse case; each should map to a mitigating requirement.

pool.q: Which requirement focuses on assigning sensitivity levels to information and evaluating the impact if it is exposed?
pool.explanation: Data classification assigns sensitivity labels and drives handling requirements (encryption, access, retention). Ownership is about accountability, anonymization is a protection technique, and replication is availability.
pool.tip: "Sensitivity level" and "impact if exposed" = classification.

pool.q: In privacy terms, who decides the purpose and means of processing personal data, versus who merely acts on instructions?
options: Data controller decides; data processor acts on instructions
pool.explanation: Under GDPR-style roles, the data controller determines the purpose and means of processing, while the data processor acts on the controller's instructions. The other pairings misstate these roles.
pool.tip: Controller = decides why/how; processor = follows instructions.

pool.q: A design collects only the personal data actually needed for the stated purpose and nothing more. Which privacy principle is this?
pool.explanation: Data minimization means collecting only what is necessary for the purpose. Purpose limitation restricts use to the stated purpose, storage limitation restricts how long data is kept, and portability is a data-subject right.
pool.tip: "Collect only what's needed" = minimization.

pool.q: A high-risk processing activity is planned for a new health platform. Which assessment is specifically intended to identify and mitigate privacy risks before processing begins?
pool.explanation: A DPIA is required for high-risk processing to identify and mitigate privacy risks up front. A BIA addresses availability/continuity impact, and the other options are security or resilience tests.
pool.tip: Privacy risk, high-risk processing, done up front = DPIA.

pool.q: Which compliance framework is written specifically for organizations that store, process, or transmit payment card data?
pool.explanation: PCI DSS is purpose-built for cardholder data security. ISO 27001 is a general ISMS framework, SOX covers financial reporting integrity, and HIPAA covers US health information.
pool.tip: Credit/debit card handling = PCI DSS.

pool.q: What is the purpose of a requirements traceability matrix (RTM) for security?
options: To map each security requirement through design, implementation, and test so nothing is missed
pool.explanation: An RTM links each requirement to its design, code, and tests, proving every security requirement was implemented and verified (and every control traces back to a requirement). It is not a runtime or reporting tool.
pool.tip: RTM proves each requirement was built AND tested.

pool.q: Pseudonymization differs from anonymization in which key way?
options: Pseudonymization is irreversible; anonymization is reversible
Pseudonymized data can be re-linked with a separately held key and remains personal data; true anonymization is irreversible
pool.explanation: Pseudonymization replaces identifiers but keeps a separate key that can re-link the data, so it is still personal data. True anonymization is irreversible and, if genuinely irreversible, is no longer personal data.
pool.tip: Re-linkable with a key = pseudonymization (still personal data).

pool.q: Which statement BEST describes the role of non-functional requirements in software behavior?
options: They define which specific features to build
They define quality attributes such as performance, scalability, security, usability, and maintainability
pool.explanation: Non-functional requirements capture quality attributes and constraints that govern how the system behaves, setting measurable targets for architecture and testing. Features are functional requirements; runbooks and staffing are operational artifacts.
pool.tip: Qualities/measurable targets = non-functional requirements.

pool.q: Which requirements should security requirements be traceable to and validated against? (Choose 2)
options: Applicable laws, regulations, and data classification
pool.explanation: Security requirements derive from sources like laws, regulations, and data classification, and must be validated against acceptance criteria and security test cases. Personal preference and budget are not sources of security requirements or validation.
pool.tip: Requirements trace up to drivers (law, classification) and down to tests.

pool.q: Privacy by default means that when a user first uses a product, the initial settings should be:
options: The most feature-rich regardless of privacy
The most privacy-protective, with sharing off unless the user opts in
pool.explanation: Privacy by default requires shipping with the most privacy-protective settings so users are protected without having to configure anything. Maximizing collection or features by default is the opposite.
pool.tip: Default settings should protect privacy, not maximize data.

pool.q: During threat modeling, a team draws a data flow diagram and pays special attention to the points where the level of trust changes, such as internet-to-web-tier. What are these points called?
pool.explanation: Trust boundaries are where trust level changes, and threats cluster there, so validation must happen at each crossing. Data stores and external entities are other DFD elements; a sequence diagram is a different model.
pool.tip: Where trust changes = trust boundary; validate at every crossing.

pool.q: In STRIDE, which threat category corresponds to a violation of integrity?
pool.explanation: In STRIDE, Tampering violates integrity. Spoofing violates authentication, Repudiation violates non-repudiation, and Denial of service violates availability.
pool.tip: STRIDE: Spoofing/auth, Tampering/integrity, Repudiation/non-rep, Info disclosure/confidentiality, DoS/availability, Elevation/authorization.

pool.q: Which threat modeling methodology is a seven-stage, risk-centric process that aligns business objectives with technical threats?
pool.explanation: PASTA (Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis) is a seven-stage, risk-centric methodology. STRIDE is a threat categorization, DREAD is a scoring model, and an attack tree enumerates attack paths.
pool.tip: Seven-stage, risk-centric, business-aligned = PASTA.

pool.q: How must user passwords be stored so that a database breach does not immediately expose them?
options: Encrypted with a symmetric key stored beside them
Hashed with a slow, salted, memory-hard algorithm such as bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2
In plaintext but behind a firewall
pool.explanation: Passwords should be hashed (not encrypted) with a slow, salted, memory-hard function so cracking is expensive and rainbow tables are defeated. Encryption with a nearby key, encoding, and plaintext all leave them recoverable.
pool.tip: Passwords are hashed (bcrypt/scrypt/Argon2 + unique salt), never encrypted.

pool.q: Why does TLS use both asymmetric and symmetric cryptography?
options: Asymmetric is used for bulk data because it is fastest
Asymmetric establishes/exchanges a symmetric session key, then fast symmetric encryption protects the bulk traffic
Symmetric is used only for signatures
It uses hashing instead of encryption
pool.explanation: Asymmetric cryptography is used to securely exchange a symmetric session key (it is too slow for bulk data); symmetric encryption then protects the actual traffic efficiently. This hybrid gets the strengths of both.
pool.tip: Asymmetric for key exchange/signatures; symmetric for bulk data.

pool.q: What does a digital signature provide, and how is it produced?
options: Confidentiality, by encrypting the whole message with a symmetric key
Integrity, authenticity, and non-repudiation, by hashing the message and encrypting the hash with the sender's private key
pool.explanation: A digital signature hashes the message and encrypts the hash with the sender's private key, giving integrity, authenticity, and non-repudiation. It is verified with the sender's public key. It does not by itself provide confidentiality.
pool.tip: Sign = hash then encrypt hash with the private key; provides integrity + authenticity + non-repudiation.

pool.q: In the cloud shared responsibility model, which items ALWAYS remain the customer's responsibility regardless of service model?
options: The customer's own data, identity/access management, and configuration
pool.explanation: The customer is always responsible for their data, IAM, and configuration; the provider secures the underlying infrastructure. Misconfigured storage buckets are the classic customer-side failure.
pool.tip: Data, identity, and configuration are always yours in the cloud.

pool.q: Which design principle favors minimizing components shared between different users or processes to avoid unintended information paths?
pool.explanation: Least common mechanism reduces mechanisms shared among users/processes to prevent covert or unintended interactions. Economy of mechanism is about overall simplicity, which is related but distinct.
pool.tip: "Don't share mechanisms between users" = least common mechanism.

pool.q: A team wants recorded TLS traffic to stay safe even if the server's long-term private key is later stolen. Which property should the design provide?
pool.explanation: Perfect forward secrecy uses ephemeral per-session keys so a future compromise of the long-term key cannot decrypt previously recorded sessions. Larger keys alone don't provide this, and disabling validation is harmful.
pool.tip: Past traffic safe after key theft = perfect forward secrecy.

pool.q: Which practice reduces a system's attack surface?
options: Enabling every optional feature by default
Disabling unused services, closing unnecessary ports, and removing default accounts
pool.explanation: Attack surface reduction removes or restricts entry points: unused services, open ports, and default accounts. Enabling everything, adding libraries, and broad admin access all enlarge the attack surface.
pool.tip: Fewer exposed features/ports/accounts = smaller attack surface.

pool.q: Which two are appropriate uses of hashing (as opposed to encryption)? (Choose 2)
options: Verifying file integrity with a digest
Providing a keyed integrity+authenticity check via HMAC
Reversibly protecting data so it can be decrypted later
pool.explanation: Hashing produces a one-way digest for integrity verification, and HMAC adds a key for integrity plus authenticity. Reversible protection and key exchange are jobs for encryption, not hashing.
pool.tip: Hashing is one-way (integrity); encryption is reversible (confidentiality).

pool.q: A microservices architecture is chosen. Which is a genuine security implication to plan for?
options: There are no network calls to secure
Many service-to-service calls need authentication and encryption (e.g., mutual TLS), and secrets must be distributed securely
Authorization can be ignored between services
The database should be exposed directly to the internet
pool.explanation: Microservices multiply network calls, so each needs authentication and encryption (mutual TLS/service mesh) and secure secret distribution; per-service authorization still matters. Ignoring these or exposing the database directly is unsafe.
pool.tip: More services = more internal calls to authenticate, encrypt, and authorize.

pool.q: A web API concatenates user input directly into a SQL query. What is the correct primary defense against SQL injection?
pool.explanation: Parameterized queries separate code from data so input can't alter query structure; combined with least-privilege DB accounts this is the fix. Output encoding addresses XSS, not SQL injection, and the other options are irrelevant.
pool.tip: SQL injection → parameterized queries / prepared statements.

pool.q: Which defense most directly prevents stored and reflected cross-site scripting (XSS)?
options: Contextual output encoding of untrusted data when rendering
pool.explanation: XSS is prevented primarily by contextual output encoding when rendering untrusted data (with CSP and HttpOnly cookies as defense in depth). Compression, server speed, and session storage don't address it.
pool.tip: XSS → contextual output encoding (+ CSP, HttpOnly).

pool.q: Why is allowlist input validation generally preferred over denylist validation?
options: Allowlists accept only known-good input, so attackers can't slip through encodings the denylist didn't anticipate
pool.explanation: Allowlists define exactly what is acceptable and reject everything else, so novel malicious encodings are blocked. Denylists must enumerate every bad pattern and inevitably miss some.
pool.tip: Allow known-good beats block known-bad.

pool.q: Input should be reduced to its simplest, standard form BEFORE validation to prevent bypasses using alternate encodings. What is this step called?
pool.explanation: Canonicalization normalizes input (resolving %2e%2e, unicode variants, path traversal sequences) so validation can't be bypassed with encoded equivalents. Validating before canonicalizing is a common flaw.
pool.tip: Canonicalize first, then validate.

pool.q: A parser reads XML from users and resolves external entities, letting an attacker read local files. What is this vulnerability, and its fix?
options: XXE; disable external entity resolution in the parser
pool.explanation: This is XML external entity (XXE) injection; the fix is to disable external entity (and DTD) processing in the XML parser. The other pairs address unrelated vulnerabilities.
pool.tip: XML + external entities reading files = XXE → disable external entities.

pool.q: An attacker changes the numeric ID in a request and retrieves another user's record because the server never checks ownership. What flaw is this?
options: Insecure direct object reference / broken object-level authorization (IDOR/BOLA)
pool.explanation: IDOR/BOLA occurs when the app trusts a client-supplied object identifier without verifying the requester is authorized for that object. The fix is per-object authorization on every request.
pool.tip: Changing an ID to read someone else's data = IDOR → enforce per-object authorization.

pool.q: Which is a correct secure session management practice?
options: Reuse the same session ID before and after login
Generate session IDs with a CSPRNG, regenerate on login, expire on logout/idle, and set Secure + HttpOnly
Put the session ID in the URL
pool.explanation: Sessions should use cryptographically random IDs, regenerate on login (defeating fixation), expire on logout and idle timeout, and travel only over TLS with Secure and HttpOnly flags. The other options enable fixation, leakage, or hijacking.
pool.tip: Regenerate on login; Secure + HttpOnly; expire on idle.

pool.q: An application returns full stack traces and database error messages to end users when something breaks. What is the security problem?
options: Information disclosure: internal details help attackers; errors should be logged server-side and shown generically to users
pool.explanation: Verbose errors leak implementation details (information disclosure). Fail securely: log details server-side and show users a generic message. It neither helps performance nor prevents injection.
pool.tip: Log details privately; show users generic errors.

pool.q: Which values must NEVER be written to application logs?
options: Passwords, session tokens, and full payment card numbers
The username performing an admin action
The name of the endpoint called
pool.explanation: Secrets such as passwords, session tokens, and full card numbers must never be logged, since logs are widely accessible and retained. Non-secret context like timestamps, actor identity, and endpoint is exactly what you should log.
pool.tip: Log the who/what/when; never log secrets.

pool.q: For generating security tokens and session identifiers, which source of randomness is appropriate?
options: A standard pseudo-random function like rand()
pool.explanation: Tokens and IDs must be unpredictable, so a CSPRNG is required. Ordinary rand(), timestamps, and counters are predictable and can be guessed or enumerated.
pool.tip: Tokens/keys/IDs → CSPRNG, never rand().

pool.q: Why combine peer code review with automated static analysis (SAST) rather than relying on one? (Choose 2)
options: Human review catches logic and business-rule flaws tools miss
SAST reliably finds every runtime configuration issue
SAST catches pattern flaws consistently across the whole codebase that humans skim past
Code review measures memory usage at runtime
pool.explanation: Humans catch logic and design flaws; SAST consistently flags known insecure patterns across all code. SAST cannot see runtime configuration, and code review does not measure runtime memory, so those statements are false.
pool.tip: People find logic flaws; tools find pattern flaws — use both.

pool.q: A shopping cart checks that an item is in stock, then a moment later completes the purchase using stale state, letting oversells happen. What class of flaw is this?
pool.explanation: A TOCTOU race condition occurs when state changes between the check and the use; the fix is atomic operations or locking and checking at time of use. The other options are unrelated flaw classes.
pool.tip: State changes between check and use = TOCTOU race condition.

pool.q: Which testing technology analyzes source code or binaries WITHOUT executing the program?
pool.explanation: SAST inspects source or compiled code without running it, giving early, broad coverage of pattern flaws. DAST tests a running app, and load and penetration testing require execution.
pool.tip: "Without executing" = static (SAST).

pool.q: A team tests the running application from the outside, like an attacker, with no source access. Which technique is this?
pool.explanation: DAST (dynamic application security testing) exercises the running app from outside and finds runtime and deployment issues. SAST and code review analyze source; threat modeling is a design activity.
pool.tip: Running app, black-box, attacker's view = DAST.

pool.q: What distinguishes generation-based (smart) fuzzing from mutation-based fuzzing?
options: Generation-based mutates random bytes with no format knowledge
Generation-based builds inputs from a protocol/format specification for deeper coverage; mutation-based mutates valid samples
pool.explanation: Generation-based fuzzing constructs inputs from a model of the format/protocol, reaching deeper states; mutation-based fuzzing mutates known-good samples without needing the spec. Neither requires source code.
pool.tip: Spec-driven inputs = generation; mutate samples = mutation.

pool.q: Vulnerability scanning and penetration testing relate how?
options: Scanning is automated breadth; penetration testing is manual depth finding chained/logic flaws; they complement each other
Scanning finds business-logic flaws better than humans
pool.explanation: Automated scanning gives broad, repeatable coverage; skilled manual penetration testing finds chained exploits and business-logic flaws scanners miss. They complement rather than replace each other.
pool.tip: Scanner = breadth (automated); pentest = depth (human).

pool.q: What must be established in writing BEFORE a penetration test begins?
options: Rules of engagement: authorization, scope, techniques, time windows, and emergency contacts
pool.explanation: Rules of engagement define written authorization, scope, permitted techniques, timing, and contacts; testing without authorization is illegal. The other documents are unrelated to test authorization.
pool.tip: No pentest without written authorization and scope (rules of engagement).

pool.q: Why should production personal data not be used directly in test environments?
options: It is too small to be useful
Test environments have weaker controls and broader access, so real data should be synthetic or masked/anonymized
It always improves test accuracy with no downside
pool.explanation: Test environments typically have weaker controls and wider access, so using raw production personal data exposes it; use synthetic or properly masked data instead. GDPR would discourage, not require, this.
pool.tip: Test data should be synthetic or masked, never raw production PII.

pool.q: Which scoring system rates a vulnerability's exploitability and impact on a 0–10 scale to help prioritize remediation?
pool.explanation: CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) scores severity 0–10 to drive prioritization; business context can adjust effective priority. SLA, RTO, and KLOC are unrelated measures.
pool.tip: 0–10 severity for prioritization = CVSS.

pool.q: After a security patch, the team re-runs previously passing tests to confirm existing functionality still works. What is this called?
pool.explanation: Regression testing verifies that recent changes did not break existing behavior (including security-relevant features). It is broader than only re-checking a single reintroduced bug.
pool.tip: "Did the change break anything that worked before?" = regression.

pool.q: What is the PRIMARY purpose of security reviews during the SDLC?
options: To validate the process and give assurance that required security controls and checkpoints are actually followed
pool.explanation: Security reviews are a process-level assurance activity confirming that requirements, design reviews, code-review policies, and gates are applied across the SDLC. They complement, not replace, technical testing.
pool.tip: Reviews assure the process and controls are followed.

pool.q: Why can high code-coverage numbers give false confidence about test quality?
options: Coverage measures which lines execute, not whether tests actually assert correct behavior
pool.explanation: Coverage tracks executed code, not the strength of assertions; tests can run code without verifying outcomes, so high coverage with weak assertions still misses defects.
pool.tip: Coverage = executed lines, not verified behavior.

pool.q: Which testing focuses on proving the system safely REJECTS invalid, unexpected, or hostile input?
pool.explanation: Negative testing feeds invalid and malicious inputs to confirm safe rejection; security testing is heavily negative testing. Happy-path testing only confirms expected inputs work.
pool.tip: Security testing leans on negative tests (safe rejection of bad input).

pool.q: Which two are runtime-oriented and therefore NOT things a purely static analyzer can reliably determine on its own? (Choose 2)
options: Presence of a hardcoded credential string
Definitive detection of all thread race conditions
Use of a disallowed API call
pool.explanation: Actual runtime memory usage and definitive race-condition detection require executing the program with real scheduling/inputs. Hardcoded strings and disallowed API usage are visible in the code and are exactly what static analysis finds.
pool.tip: Runtime behavior (memory, races) needs execution; code patterns are static.

pool.q: Which set of actions best describes hardening a server before deployment?
options: Enable all services and keep default passwords for convenience
Disable unused services and ports, change default credentials, and apply a secure baseline (e.g., CIS Benchmarks)
Turn off logging to save space
pool.explanation: Hardening removes or disables what isn't needed, changes defaults, and applies a known secure baseline. The other options enlarge the attack surface or remove needed visibility.
pool.tip: Hardening = remove defaults/unused services, apply a secure baseline.

pool.q: Where should an application obtain database passwords and API keys at runtime?
options: From a secrets manager / vault, injected at runtime, never committed to the repo or image
pool.explanation: Secrets should come from a vault/secrets manager at runtime and be rotated and audited; hardcoding or committing them leaks through repositories and images. Public files and email are obviously unsafe.
pool.tip: Secrets live in a vault, never in code or images.

pool.q: List the phases of incident response in the correct order.
options: Detection, Preparation, Recovery, Containment, Eradication, Lessons learned
Preparation, Detection & analysis, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, Lessons learned
Containment, Preparation, Recovery, Detection, Lessons learned, Eradication
Recovery, Containment, Detection, Preparation, Eradication, Lessons learned
pool.explanation: The standard order is Preparation; Detection & analysis; Containment; Eradication; Recovery; Lessons learned. Preparation happens before any incident, and lessons learned closes the loop.
pool.tip: Prepare → Detect → Contain → Eradicate → Recover → Learn.

pool.q: What is the difference between containment and eradication in incident response?
options: Containment stops the spread (isolate hosts, block indicators); eradication removes the root cause (malware, attacker access, vulnerable component)
pool.explanation: Containment limits damage and spread first; eradication then removes the underlying cause so the threat can't persist. Restoration is part of recovery, not containment.
pool.tip: Contain = stop the bleeding; eradicate = remove the cause.

pool.q: Define RTO and RPO.
options: RTO is max tolerable data loss; RPO is max tolerable downtime
RTO is max tolerable downtime; RPO is max tolerable data loss (in time), which drives backup frequency
pool.explanation: RTO (recovery time objective) is the maximum tolerable downtime; RPO (recovery point objective) is the maximum tolerable data loss measured in time, which sets how often backups must run.
pool.tip: RTO = time to restore; RPO = how much data (time) you can lose.

pool.q: Which backup practice best protects against ransomware that encrypts online systems?
options: Keep a single backup on the same server
Follow a 3-2-1 approach with at least one offline/offsite copy, encrypt backups, and test restores regularly
Never test restores to save time
Store backups only on the production SAN
pool.explanation: 3-2-1 (multiple copies, different media, one offline/offsite), encrypted and restore-tested, ensures a clean recovery point ransomware can't reach. Online-only or same-server backups can be encrypted along with production.
pool.tip: 3-2-1 + offline copy + tested restores beats ransomware.

pool.q: Why is the CI/CD pipeline itself treated as production-critical infrastructure to secure?
options: It is only used for documentation
Whoever controls the pipeline can inject code into every release, so it needs signed commits/artifacts, restricted credentials, and integrity checks
It has no access to secrets
pool.explanation: The pipeline builds and ships everything, so compromising it poisons all downstream releases; it must be hardened with signing, restricted credentials, and integrity checks. It clearly does affect what ships and handles secrets.
pool.tip: Control the pipeline = control every release; protect it accordingly.

pool.q: Production has slowly diverged from the approved secure baseline over months of ad-hoc changes. What is this condition, and how is it detected?
options: Configuration drift; detected by periodic compliance scans and re-applying infrastructure-as-code
Denial of service; detected by load balancers
pool.explanation: Configuration drift is divergence from the baseline over time; periodic compliance scanning and re-applying infrastructure-as-code detect and correct it. The other options describe unrelated problems.
pool.tip: Drift = baseline erosion over time → scan and re-apply IaC.

pool.q: What is the essential goal of a vulnerability management cycle?
options: To run once per year and file a report
To continuously discover, assess/prioritize (CVSS + context), remediate or mitigate, verify, and report
To only patch after a breach
pool.explanation: Vulnerability management is continuous: discover, prioritize with CVSS plus business context, remediate or apply compensating controls, verify, and report. Annual-only or reactive-only approaches leave long exposure windows.
pool.tip: Vuln management is a continuous loop, not an annual event.

pool.q: What is the correct approach to patch management for a known-exploited vulnerability?
options: Deploy untested patches straight to production immediately
Test in staging, then deploy promptly by risk priority, with an emergency path for actively exploited flaws
Wait for the annual maintenance window regardless
Ignore it if there is a firewall
pool.explanation: Patches should be tested in staging then deployed promptly by risk, with an expedited emergency process for actively exploited flaws. Unpatched known vulnerabilities are among the most common breach causes.
pool.tip: Test in staging, patch by risk, expedite actively-exploited flaws.

pool.q: Why should each application or service run under its own least-privilege service account rather than a shared one?
options: A shared account destroys accountability and widens the blast radius if compromised
It removes the need for logging
pool.explanation: Per-service least-privilege accounts preserve accountability (actions trace to one service) and limit damage if credentials leak. A shared account can't be attributed and grants broad access on compromise.
pool.tip: Unique least-privilege accounts = accountability + smaller blast radius.

pool.q: Which items belong in an incident response plan BEFORE an incident, beyond technical steps? (Choose 2)
options: Communication paths and legal/regulatory breach-notification duties
pool.explanation: An IR plan must predefine communications, legal/regulatory notification timelines, and evidence preservation with chain of custody, so they aren't improvised mid-incident. Marketing and lunch are irrelevant.
pool.tip: Pre-plan comms, notification duties, and evidence handling.

pool.q: When a new vulnerability is disclosed in a popular library, which artifact lets a team instantly answer "are we affected, and where?"
pool.explanation: An SBOM is a complete inventory of every component and version in a product, enabling rapid impact analysis when a component vulnerability appears. The other artifacts don't inventory dependencies.
pool.tip: "Are we affected?" answered fast = SBOM.

pool.q: What is the PRIMARY objective of secure software supply chain practices?
options: To lower the chance of incorporating tampered or malicious third-party components
To guarantee compliance with every regulation
To reduce the number of developers
pool.explanation: Supply chain security aims to prevent compromised or malicious dependencies from entering builds and releases, via SBOMs, signing, reproducible builds, and dependency scanning. Speed and full compliance are not the core goal.
pool.tip: Supply chain security = keep tampered/malicious components out.

pool.q: An attacker publishes a package to a public registry using the same name as a company's internal package but a higher version number, hoping the build pulls theirs. What attack is this?
pool.explanation: Dependency confusion abuses resolvers that prefer higher versions across public and private registries, tricking builds into pulling the attacker's public package. Typosquatting instead relies on misspelled names.
pool.tip: Same internal name, higher public version = dependency confusion.

pool.q: What are reproducible builds, and why do they matter for supply chain security?
options: Builds that run faster each time
Independent builds from the same source that produce bit-identical artifacts, proving the build wasn't tampered with
Builds that only run in the cloud
pool.explanation: Reproducible builds let independent parties rebuild from source and get identical binaries, so tampering during the build is detectable. Speed, skipping tests, and cloud location are unrelated.
pool.tip: Bit-identical rebuilds prove no build tampering.

pool.q: A company vets a critical software vendor before purchase. Which evidence is MOST relevant to the vendor's security posture?
options: Certifications and reports such as ISO 27001 and SOC 2, plus secure-development evidence and pentest summaries
The number of social media followers
pool.explanation: Security assessments rely on certifications (ISO 27001), attestations (SOC 2), and evidence of secure development and testing. Location, branding, and social metrics don't indicate security posture.
pool.tip: Vendor assurance = ISO 27001 / SOC 2 / pentest evidence.

pool.q: How do copyleft licenses (e.g., GPL) differ from permissive licenses (e.g., MIT/BSD/Apache 2.0) in a way that matters for shipping proprietary software?
options: Strong copyleft can require derivative works to be released under the same license, whereas permissive licenses generally allow proprietary use with attribution
Permissive licenses forbid all commercial use
pool.explanation: Strong copyleft (GPL) can force derivative works to adopt the same license, a real risk for proprietary products if linked improperly; permissive licenses allow proprietary use with attribution (Apache 2.0 also grants patents). An SBOM should track licenses, not just versions.
pool.tip: GPL can be "viral"; MIT/BSD/Apache are permissive.

pool.q: A trusted open-source package is hijacked and a malicious version is published. Which practice best mitigates blindly adopting it?
options: Always auto-update to the latest version instantly
Pin dependency versions and review updates (and monitor for maintainer/account compromise) before adopting
Trust any signed package without checking the signer
pool.explanation: Version pinning plus reviewing updates prevents automatically pulling a hijacked release, and monitoring catches maintainer compromise. Blind auto-update and disabling scanning increase risk.
pool.tip: Pin versions and review updates; don't blindly auto-adopt.

pool.q: What distinguishes provenance from pedigree for a software component?
options: Pedigree is where a component came from and how it was built; provenance is the verifiable chain of custody from source to artifact
pool.explanation: Pedigree describes origin and build history; provenance is the verifiable, tamper-evident chain of custody from source to the final artifact. Verifying checksums and signatures supports provenance.
pool.tip: Pedigree = origin/history; provenance = verifiable chain of custody.

pool.q: How should software updates be delivered to prevent attackers from pushing malicious updates through the update channel?
options: Over plain HTTP with no verification
Sign updates and verify signatures before install, protecting the signing keys (ideally in an HSM)
Let any server on the network push updates
pool.explanation: Signed updates verified before installation, with signing keys protected in an HSM, prevent update-channel hijacking. Unsigned/unverified delivery is exactly what attackers exploit.
pool.tip: Sign updates, verify before install, protect the signing keys.

pool.q: What is the ongoing nature of third-party/vendor risk management?
options: A one-time check at purchase is sufficient
It is continuous: re-assess periodically and on triggers such as a breach at the vendor, ownership change, or EOL announcement
It ends once the contract is signed
pool.explanation: Vendor posture changes over time, so risk must be re-assessed periodically and on trigger events. A single purchase-time check leaves emerging risks unmanaged.
pool.tip: Vendor risk is continuous, not one-and-done.

pool.q: Which continuous practice identifies known-vulnerable open-source components in your dependencies?
pool.explanation: SCA scans dependencies against vulnerability databases (and flags license issues) continuously. Load testing, UX research, and chaos engineering address other concerns.
pool.tip: Scanning dependencies for known CVEs = SCA.

pool.q: Which two are sound criteria when selecting a third-party component to depend on? (Choose 2)
options: It is actively maintained and responsive to security reports
It has the most transitive dependencies possible
Its license is compatible with your product
It was last updated many years ago
pool.explanation: Prefer actively maintained, security-responsive components with a compatible license. Maximizing transitive dependencies enlarges risk, and abandoned (long-unmaintained) components are a silent hazard.
pool.tip: Pick maintained, security-responsive, license-compatible components.
